literal: clearness, serenity, graciousness, favor; action-noun from pra- ('forth, completely') + √sad 'to settle, to sink down, to become clear' (pra-√sad + ghañ → prasāda), 'a settling-down' — hence the clearing of a turbid mind (as silt settles and water grows clear), and, of a superior, the gracious favor that 'settles' kindly upon another
invariant_definition: *Prasāda* has two closely related technical senses that share the image of a 'settling' encoded in pra-√sad. The first is the clearness or serenity of mind: as silt settles and turbid water becomes transparent, the agitated mind, freed of the disturbance of attraction and aversion, becomes calm and lucid. This is the sense in the *sthitaprajña* discourse, where one who moves among objects with senses free of *rāga* and *dveṣa* 'attains *prasāda*' (2.64), and 'in *prasāda* all his sorrows are destroyed, for the intellect of the clear-minded (*prasanna-cetas*) soon becomes firmly fixed' (2.65). Śaṅkara glosses *prasāda* here as *prasannatā*, *svāsthya*, the clarity and self-poise of the inner organ — the medium in which steady wisdom (*buddhi*) establishes itself. The second sense is gracious favor: the kindly disposition of a deity, teacher, or superior that 'settles' upon the recipient, as in *tat-prasādāt* ('by his grace'). In the theistic readings the two senses join: the serenity of mind is itself the fruit of the Lord's grace. *Prasāda* is thus the calm-and-clarity that is at once a psychological condition (the settled mind) and, in devotional registers, a gift bestowed.

school_framing.shared_core: *Prasāda* names **the clearing and settling of the mind into serenity and lucidity — turbidity giving way to transparency**; in devotional and theistic registers the same word names the gracious favor that bestows or effects that clarity. The 'settling' image is common to both; what varies is whether the clarity is achieved by self-discipline or received as grace.
school_framing.register_axes_note: **Same concept, different aspect** between the psychological use (mental clarity won by withdrawing the senses from *rāga-dveṣa*, the Gītā's 2.64-65) and the devotional use (clarity as *anugraha*, the Lord's grace). Madhva, reading 2.55, ties the serenity (*tuṣṭi*) of the knower to *tat-prasādāt* — the grace of the Lord abiding within — so that for him the mind's *prasāda* is inseparable from divine favor; Śaṅkara takes *prasāda* at 2.64-65 as the *svacchatā* of the inner organ, the lucidity in which steady *buddhi* arises.

per_school.definition: In the *sthitaprajña* discourse *prasāda* is the clarity of mind reached by one whose senses, freed of attraction and aversion, no longer disturb the inner organ (2.64); in this clarity all sorrows are destroyed and the intellect of the clear-minded (*prasanna-cetas*) becomes firmly fixed (2.65). Śaṅkara glosses *prasāda* as *prasannatā* and *svāsthya* — the lucid self-poise of the *antaḥkaraṇa* — and Madhusūdana as *cittasya svacchatā*, the transparency in which *brahma-jñāna* can settle; the destruction of sorrow follows because in that clarity the discriminating *buddhi* stabilizes and the ignorance opposed to it falls away.
primary_loci: Bhagavad-Gītā 2.65 (prasāde sarva-duḥkhānāṃ hānir ... prasanna-cetasaḥ ... buddhiḥ paryavatiṣṭhate)
per_school.register_tag: M+S; mumukṣu (prasāda as clarity of antaḥkaraṇa)

per_school.definition: Madhva foregrounds the second sense: reading 2.55, he ties the knower's serenity (*tuṣṭi*) to *tat-prasādāt* — it arises by the grace of the Lord (the *paramātman* / Nārāyaṇa-Rāma) abiding within, not by the self's own resources alone. Self-knowledge, contentment, and liberation all depend on Viṣṇu's *anugraha*; the mind's *prasāda* is accordingly inseparable from divine favor, so that for him the psychological clarity and the bestowed grace are two aspects of one gift.
primary_loci: the Nārāyaṇa-Rāma-kalpa citation on devotion and divine favor (ad 2.55)

textual_history.summary: *Prasāda* ('clearness, serenity; grace, favour', from pra-√sad, 'to settle, become clear') runs along a double sense from its Vedic-Upaniṣadic roots into the *Gītā*. In the Kaṭha Upaniṣad the self is seen 'by the grace/clearness of the Ordainer' (*dhātu-prasādāt*) — a phrase poised between subjective serenity and a granted grace. The *Gītā* foregrounds the *serenity* sense: the disciplined person 'attains clearness/serenity' of mind (*prasādam adhigacchati*, 2.64), and in that clearness (*prasāde*, 2.65) all sorrows are destroyed. The double sense — clearness-of-mind and grace-bestowed — is original to the term and never fully resolves; the *Gītā* leans toward serenity, the theistic Upaniṣads and later bhakti toward grace.

stages.sanskrit: tam akratuḥ paśyati vītaśoko dhātuprasādān mahimānam ātmanaḥ
stages.rendering: Free of grief, the desireless one beholds the greatness of the self by the grace/clearness of the Ordainer (dhātu-prasādāt).
stages.note: The earliest pole and the locus of the double sense. *Dhātu-prasādāt* is read two ways: 'by the grace of the Ordainer (dhātṛ)' — a granted favour — and 'by the serenity/clearness of the faculties (dhātu)' — an achieved calm. The grief-free, desireless seer beholds the self's greatness; whether by grace from above or by clarity within is exactly the ambiguity the *Gītā* later inherits. (The su-/serene grace-language recurs in the theistic Śvetāśvatara.)

stages.sanskrit: rāga-dveṣa-viyuktais tu viṣayān indriyaiś caran | ātma-vaśyair vidheyātmā prasādam adhigacchati ||
stages.rendering: But one of disciplined self, ranging over the sense-objects with senses freed from attraction and aversion, brought under control — he attains serenity (prasādam adhigacchati).
stages.note: The *Gītā* foregrounds the *clearness/serenity* sense. Śaṅkara glosses 'prasādaḥ prasannatā svāsthyam' — 'prasāda is serenity, well-standing-in-oneself' — an achieved inner calm, not a granted favour. The self-controlled person 'attains prasāda', mental clarity won through detachment from rāga and dveṣa.

stages.sanskrit: prasāde sarva-duḥkhānāṃ hānir asyopajāyate | prasanna-cetaso hy āśu buddhiḥ paryavatiṣṭhate ||
stages.rendering: In serenity (prasāde) the destruction of all his sorrows arises; for the intellect of one whose mind is clear (prasanna-cetas) is soon firmly established.
stages.note: The fruit of *prasāda*: in the clarified mind all sorrows are destroyed and the intellect steadies. The repetition prasāde / prasanna-cetas seals the *Gītā*'s preferred sense — *prasāda* as the limpid, sorrow-dissolving calm of the controlled mind. The cognate *manaḥ-prasāda* ('clarity of mind') reappears among the mental austerities at 17.16.

translator_note: Two renderings are needed and should not be conflated. In the *sthitaprajña* context *prasāda* is 'clarity' or 'serenity' of mind — the lucidity of a settled *antaḥkaraṇa*, captured by the silt-settling image of pra-√sad; render it so, not as 'grace', at 2.64-65. In devotional and theistic contexts (and in Madhva's reading of 2.55) the same word means 'grace' or 'favor' — the kindly disposition of a deity or teacher that settles upon the recipient. The English 'grace' imports Christian connotations that should be handled with care; *prasāda* is at root a clearing-and-settling, and the gift-sense grows out of that. Avoid the food-offering sense (*prasāda* as consecrated remnants), which is a later ritual extension irrelevant here.
